content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                    Date : July 23, 1979

                                            A 79-096

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Sarah Cameron [signature]

Subject: Conversation with Ruth Frishman, Emeryville Redevelopment Agency


In response to the attached letter, I called Ruth Frishman on July 20, 1979. I apologized for the delay in responding and asked her what the situation was. She said that the "leveraging" is that the City deposits $175,000 with Crocker Bank and they in turn make loans of approximately $300,000 to qualified owners. In addition, there is money from the feds which is directly available to owners through programs administered by the County with some participation by the City. A City Councilmember and the City Clerk were applying for such loans. Ms. Frishman wanted to know whether there was any problem in granting the applications.

In light of our letter to Preston Hill, dated Nov. 3, 1978, I told Ms. Frishman that there were no problems under the PRA if neither woman used her official position to influence the decisions on the applications. In addition, I said, they should not participate in decisions relating to the loan program while they have loans outstanding and they must report the loans on their Statements.

Emeryville Redevelopment Agency

5720 Hollis Street • Emeryville, California 94608 • (415) 658-8901

WALLACE E. FOX
CHAIRMAN

WYLIE EATON
VICE-CHAIRMAN

RICHARD KASSIS
COUNCIL MEMBER

RENA RICKLES
COUNCIL MEMBER

21 May 1979

ESTHER SPRIGGS
COUNCIL MEMBER

MARK BUELL
EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR

State Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear People:

We have a potential conflict of interest problem with two of
our loan applications.

The two applicants are Roberta Horton, the City Clerk and
Esther Spriggs, a City Councilwoman and member of the Re-
development Agency.

They have applied for the HUD Section 312 rehab loan. As an
alternative, they have been considered for the private rehab
loan program that we leveraged with HCD and Redevelopment funds
through Crocker Bank.

Please let us know if there is any conflict of interest problem
with either of these programs and/or any public hearing pro-
cedures that should be initiated before qualifying either
applicant for a loan.

Thank you for your assistance.

Sincerely,

Ruth I. Frishman
Housing Specialist

RIF:mb

sections.question: We have a potential conflict of interest problem with two of our loan applications. The two applicants are Roberta Horton, the City Clerk and Esther Spriggs, a City Councilwoman and member of the Redevelopment Agency. They have applied for the HUD Section 312 rehab loan. As an alternative, they have been considered for the private rehab loan program that we leveraged with HCD and Redevelopment funds through Crocker Bank. Please let us know if there is any conflict of interest problem with either of these programs and/or any public hearing procedures that should be initiated before qualifying either applicant for a loan.
sections.conclusion: In light of our letter to Preston Hill, dated Nov. 3, 1978, I told Ms. Frishman that there were no problems under the PRA if neither woman used her official position to influence the decisions on the applications. In addition, I said, they should not participate in decisions relating to the loan program while they have loans outstanding and they must report the loans on their Statements.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether there is a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act if a City Clerk and City Councilwoman/Redevelopment Agency member apply for rehabilitation loans administered by the City and Redevelopment Agency.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: No conflict of interest exists under the Political Reform Act if the applicants do not use their official positions to influence loan decisions. However, they must recuse themselves from participating in loan program decisions while their loans are outstanding and must report the loans on their financial statements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (dated May 21, 1979) and the FPPC's memorandum response (dated July 23, 1979) documenting a telephone conversation. No formal Q/C sections with headers, but question and conclusion are clearly identifiable from the letter and memorandum content. Document ID 79-096 references prior FPPC letter dated Nov. 3, 1978.

The two applicants are Roberta Horton, the City Clerk and Esther Spriggs, a City Councilwoman and member of the Redevelopment Agency. They have applied for the HUD Section 312 rehab loan. As an alternative, they have been considered for the private rehab loan program that we leveraged with HCD and Redevelopment funds through Crocker Bank. Please let us know if there is any conflict of interest problem with either of these programs and/or any public hearing procedures that should be initiated before qualifying either applicant for a loan.

CONCLUSION: In light of our letter to Preston Hill, dated Nov. 3, 1978, I told Ms. Frishman that there were no problems under the PRA if neither woman used her official position to influence the decisions on the applications. In addition, I said, they should not participate in decisions relating to the loan program while they have loans outstanding and they must report the loans on their Statements.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responded to an inquiry from the Emeryville Redevelopment Agency regarding potential conflicts of interest for a City Clerk and City Councilwoman who applied for rehabilitation loans. The FPPC advised that no conflict exists provided the applicants do not use their positions to influence decisions and comply with recusal and reporting requirements.
